zh.lifeSummary: Lady Annah "Anne" James, Baroness Hungerford and Percy於1454年出生在英國越可爾萊康費爾德。她於大約1475年與Sir Peter Percy在英國越可爾結婚。他們至少是6個兒子的父母。她去世於1512年5月22日在英國英格蘭越可爾享年58歲，埋葬在英國英格蘭越可爾。

nameMeanings.name: James,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English and Welsh: from the Middle English personal name James. Introduced to England by the Normans, this is an Old French form of Late Latin Iacomus, a variant of Latin Iacobus, Greek Iakōbos, the New Testament rendering of Hebrew Ya‘aqob (see Jacob ). The medieval Latin (Vulgate) Bible distinguished between Old Testament Iacob (which was uninflected) and New Testament Iacobus (with inflections). The latter developed into James in medieval French. The distinction was carried over into the King James Bible of 1611, and Jacob and James remain as separate names in English usage. Most European languages, however, make no such distinction, so that forms such as French Jacques , stand for both the Old and the New Testament names. This surname is also very common among African Americans. Compare Jack .

variants.name: Giacomo
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Domenico, Gino, Salvatore.
Italian: from the personal name Giacomo, from Latin Jacobus via Late Latin Jacomus (see James and Jacob ).

variants.name: Jamie
meanings: Scottish (mainly Angus): from a Scots pet form of the personal name   James  .

variants.name: Jemison
meanings: English (northern): patronymic from the personal name Jem (a pet form of James ) + son. Compare Jamison .

variants.name: Jemmett
meanings: English (southern): from the Middle English personal name Jemet, a diminutive of Jem, a pet form of James .

variants.name: Jaime
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Jesus, Jorge, Juan, Manuel, Carlos, Javier, Arturo, Luis, Alfredo, Enrique, Miguel.
Spanish: from the personal name Jaime, an equivalent of James .

variants.name: Jamison
meanings: English and Scottish: from Middle English and Scots Jami(e)son, Jameson, Jem(i)son ‘son of James’ (see James ). Jameson and Jamison are mainly found in northern England, especially in Northumberland and Durham, while in Scotland the more usual form is Jamieson , but all three versions occur on either side of the border.
Scottish: variant of   McCamish  .

variants.name: Jacobo
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Jose, Jesus, Francisco, Juan, Manuel, Miguel, Rafael, Alfredo, Luis, Carlos, Mario, Roberto.
Spanish and Italian: from the Latin personal name Jacobus (see James , Giacomo ).

variants.name: Jemmott
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Spanish Alicia, Armando, Dionicio, Gregoria, Guillermo, Roberto, Rosita, Ruperto.
English: from the personal name Jemot, a diminutive of Jem, a pet form of James . This surname is frequent in Barbados, Trinidad and Tobago, and Saint Lucia.

variants.name: Como
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Sal, Salvatore, Giuseppe, Ignazio, Nunzio, Vito, Angelo, Antonio, Camillo, Carlo, Carmela, Caterina.
Italian: from the personal name Como, a short form of Giacomo, an equivalent of James .
Italian: habitational name from Como in Lombardy.

variants.name: Jacob
meanings: Jewish, English, Welsh, German, Portuguese, French (mainly Alsace and Lorraine), Walloon, Breton, Dutch, Flemish, Swedish, Danish, and Norwegian: derivative, via Latin Jacob(us), from the Hebrew personal name Ya‘aqob (Yaakov). In the Bible, this is the name of the crafty younger twin brother of Esau (Genesis 25:26), who took advantage of the latter's hunger and impetuousness to persuade him to part with his birthright ‘for a mess of potage’. The name is traditionally interpreted as coming from Hebrew akev ‘heel’: Jacob is said to have been born holding on to Esau's heel. In English usage the name Jacob is regarded as distinct from the name James , but they are of identical origin. In North America, the English form of the surname has absorbed cognates from other languages, for example Assyrian/Chaldean or Arabic Yaqub , Yakub , Yacoub , or Yacob , Slovenian Jakob and Jakop, Czech and Slovak Jakub , and also their patronymics and other derivatives (see examples at Jacobs and Jacobson ). The name Jacob is also found among Christians in southern India (compare Chacko ), but since South Indians traditionally do not have hereditary surnames, the southern Indian name was in most cases registered as such only after immigration of its bearers to the US.

nameMeanings.name: Baroness
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Scandinavian Nels.

Norwegian: old variant of Barsnes (and, in North America, probably also an altered form of this), a habitational name from a farmstead so named in the county of Sogn og Fjordane, possibly from a compound of the Old Norse personal name Baldrekr + nes ‘point, headland’.

nameMeanings.name: Hungerford
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: habitational name from Hungerford (Berkshire), from Old English hungor ‘hunger’, here probably denoting unproductive land, + ford. This surname has been established in Ireland since the 17th century.

nameMeanings.name: and
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Altered form of French Canadian Hénault or directly of French Hunault (see Henault 2).
Catalan (Añó): Castilianized form of Anyó, probably from the old personal name Annone.
Japanese: variant of   Anno  .

variants.name: Anno
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Japanese Masayuki, Tadashi, Yoshito.
Japanese: written 安野 ‘peaceful plain’. This surname is rare in Japan proper, and may have originated in the Ryūkyū Islands, where it is pronounced   Ano  .

variants.name: Henault
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Armand, Andre, Emile, Lucien, Martial, Pierre, Camille, Fernand, Laurier, Monique, Normand, Philippe.
French (Hénault): from the ancient Germanic personal name Haginwald, composed of the elements hagin ‘enclosure, protected place’ and wald ‘power, authority’.
Altered form (Hénault) of French Hunault: from the ancient Germanic personal name Hunwald, composed of the elements hun ‘bear cub’ and wald ‘power, authority’. Compare Ano and Eno .

nameMeanings.name: Percy
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (of Norman origin):

nickname from Old French percehaie ‘pierce hedge’ (Old French percer ‘to pierce, penetrate’ + haie ‘hedge, fence’), perhaps with the sense of someone breaking into an enclosure. Percehaie is the name of one of the sons of the foxes Renart and Hermeline in the medieval French epic Roman de Renart, whose earliest known version is from the 1170s. The surname is older than that, but it may originate in a nickname for the fox as ‘enclosure piercer’, perhaps amounting to ‘chicken thief’.

habitational name from any of several places called Percy in Calvados, Eure, and Manche; William de Perci, the Domesday tenant-in-chief and under-tenant of Hugh, Earl of Chester, came from either Percy-en-Auge (Eure) or Percy (Manche).

variants.name: Piercey
meanings: English (of Norman origin): variant of   Percy  .

variants.name: Piercy
meanings: English (of Norman origin): variant of   Percy  .

variants.name: Pearcey
meanings: English: variant of   Percy  .

variants.name: Peercy
meanings: English: variant of   Percy  .

variants.name: Pearcy
meanings: English: variant of   Percy  .

nameMeanings.name: Lady
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: from Middle English lavedi, levedi, ladi ‘lady, female head of a household, woman, wife’ (Old English hlǣfdige). As a man's name it may have been used in mockery, or be short for occupational names such as Ladyman.
Polish: variant of   Lada  .
Hungarian (Ládi): habitational name for someone from Lád in Borsod County or Lad in Somogy County.

variants.name: Lada
meanings: ‘Morning dew’; also ‘generosity’.

nameMeanings.name: Annah
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Biblical name, borne by the mother of the prophet Samuel (1 Samuel 1:2), Hebrew Hanna. It is derived from a Hebrew word meaning ‘He (i.e. God) has favoured me (i.e. with a child)’. See also Anne . This form of the name was taken up as a given name by the Puritans in the 16th and 17th centuries and remained popular until the late 19th century. Thereafter it fell somewhat from favour but has enjoyed a massive revival since the 1990s.

variants.name: Hanna
meanings: Either the biblical name ( see Hannah ) or a short form of Johanna .

variants.name: Hana
meanings: Originally a feminine form of John , from the Old French form Je(h)anne. Since the 17th century it has proved the most popular of the feminine forms of John, ahead of Joan and Jean . It now also commonly occurs as the second element in combinations such as Sarah-Jane. In Britain it is still one of the most frequent of all girls' names. It is not a royal name, but was borne by the tragic Lady Jane Grey ( 1537–54 ), who was unwillingly proclaimed queen in 1553 , deposed nine days later, and executed the following year. Seventy years earlier, the name had come into prominence as that of Jane Shore , mistress of King Edward IV and subsequently of Thomas Grey , 1st Marquess of Dorset, Lady Jane's grandfather. Jane Shore's tribulations in 1483 at the hands of Richard III , Edward's brother and successor, became the subject of popular ballads and plays, which may well have increased the currency of the name in the 16th century. A 19th-century influence was its use as the name of the central character in Charlotte Brontë's novel Jane Eyre ( 1847 ). From 1932 to 1959 it was used as the name of a cheerful and scantily clad beauty whose adventures were chronicled in a strip cartoon in the Daily Mirror. It is also borne by the American film stars Jane Russell ( 1921–2011 ) and Jane Fonda ( b. 1937 ).

nameMeanings.name: "Anne"
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: variant of Anne , in common use as a given name in most European languages. Among people with a classical education, it has from time to time been associated with Virgil's Aeneid, where it is borne by the sister of Dido , Queen of Carthage. This Phoenician name may ultimately be of Semitic origin, and thus related to the biblical Anne. However, the connection, if it exists, is indirect rather than direct.

variants.name: Anne
meanings: variant of Anne , in common use as a given name in most European languages. Among people with a classical education, it has from time to time been associated with Virgil's Aeneid, where it is borne by the sister of Dido , Queen of Carthage. This Phoenician name may ultimately be of Semitic origin, and thus related to the biblical Anne. However, the connection, if it exists, is indirect rather than direct.

historicEvents.title: Wars of the Roses
historicEvents.description: Wars of the Roses was a series of English civil wars for control of the throne of England between the royal families House of Lancaster and the House of York. The name of the war was derived as the red rose was the symbol of the Lancastrians and the white rose was the symbol of the Yorks.

en.lifeSummary: Lady Annah "Anne" James, Baroness Hungerford and Percy was born in 1454, in Leconfield, Yorkshire, England. She married Sir Peter Percy about 1475, in Yorkshire, England. They were the parents of at least 6 sons. She died on 22 May 1512, in Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 58, and was buried in Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom.

Ils ont eu au moins 6 fils. Elle est décédée le 22 mai 1512, à Everwyck, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 58 ans, et a été enterrée à Everwyck, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni.

ja.lifeSummary: Lady Annah "Anne" James, Baroness Hungerford and Percyは1454年にイギリスヨークシュアイアレコンフィールドで生まれた。約1475年にイギリスヨークシュアイアでSir Peter Percyと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも6人の息子の親であった。彼女は1512年5月22日にイギリスイギリスヨークシュアイアで58歳で死去し，イギリスイギリスヨークシュアイアにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: Lady Annah "Anne" James, Baroness Hungerford and Percy(은)는 1454년 영국 요르크실르 렉필드에서 태어났다. 그녀는 대략 1475년 영국 요르크실르에서 Sir Peter Percy(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 여섯 명의 아들을 두었다. 그녀는 1512년 5월 22일 영국 잉글랜드 요르크실르에서 58세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 잉글랜드 요르크실르에 묻혔다.
